segments.s758-g25: ببستش is past-tense + enclitic object (ش) meaning “he bound/tied it.” بران here functions as a deictic/relational particle “on/onto that,” followed by باره “a burden/load” and بر “on,” with همچوسنگ a simile meaning “like a stone.” The phrase as a whole describes fastening the object and placing a stone-like weight upon it (word order: verb + object-clitic, then locative/qualifying phrase).
segments.s758-g26: فگنده is a past-participial/preterite form meaning “thrown” (here describing the action of casting the weighted thing). به + گردن marks the target/location “to/around the neck,” and درون پالهنگ names whose neck it is (the leopard). The sequence puts the throwing action after the placing: “having thrown (it) onto/around the leopard’s neck.”
segments.s758-g27: چنان here is a demonstrative/intensifier “such/so,” modifying the following noun. کره is the noun “calf,” with the adjectives تیز “swift” and نادیده “unseen” qualifying it; classical Persian often places adjectives after the noun. زین “saddle” names the place/source (literally ‘from the saddle’), forming the noun phrase “that swift, unseen calf from the saddle.”
segments.s758-g28: به میدان کشید is a verbal clause: به + میدان marks direction/goal “into the field,” and کشید (past of کشیدن) means “drew/pulled” (here ‘drew out’). آن خداوند کین is the agent phrase “that lord/master of vengeance” performing the action. Note: classical ordering puts the verb before the agent phrase in some lines.
segments.s758-g29: سواران is the plural subject “horsemen/riders.” به میدان فرستاد is a simple past clause “(he) sent to the field”; چند after فرستاد qualifies the sent force as “several/a few.” Word order is subject then verb phrase, with چند as an adverbial quantifier.
segments.s758-g30: This clause describes their equipment: به فتراک “on/at the saddle-cloth” (به + noun marking accompaniment/attaching), بر گرد کرده is a verbal phrase meaning “having made/put around” (بر + گرد ‘around’ + کرده as a perfect/participial sense), and کمند is the object “lasso/sling.” Together: ‘with lassos made/looped about the saddle-cloth.’
segments.s758-g31: که تا introduces a subordinate clause with a sense close to “so that/whenever.” کره او را is the marked object phrase “his calf” (او = ‘his’, را = object marker). همی‌تاختی is the imperfective/past habitual form with the continuous prefix همی-: “you would charge/attack.” Together it gives the idea “so that whenever you charged at his calf...”
segments.s758-g32: زمان تا زمانش is a repetitive/time phrase meaning “time to time / from time to time” (literally ‘time until his time’ used idiomatically). بینداختی is the past tense “you threw (down)/you cast (off).” The clause states the habitual result: “you would, now and then, throw him down.”
segments.s758-g41: چو introduces a temporal clause meaning “when/after.” شش ماه بگذشت is a past narrative: شش ماه (six months) + بگذشت (passed). بر کار اوی is a prepositional phrase ‘in/on his affairs’ (بر + کار) where اوی is the archaic/poetic third-person possessive clitic meaning “his.” Word order follows classical Persian: temporal introducer → duration + verb → locational/possessive phrase.
segments.s758-g42: ببد is a narrative past/preterite form meaning “became/turned” (here “turned”); ناگهان is an adverb “suddenly.” کژ is an adjective “crooked/askew” modifying پرگار “compass/drawing‑instrument,” and the final اوی is again the possessive clitic “his.” The verb precedes the adverbial and adjectival phrase in this poetic ordering.
segments.s758-g43: به یک هفته here gives the timespan ‘in/within one week’ (به used adverbially). بیمار گشت uses گشتن in the stative sense ‘became’ (he became sick), and و بمرد connects the next action ‘and he died’ (بمرد is a classical narrative past form meaning ‘died’). The conjunction و links the two past events.
segments.s758-g44: ابا is a verb meaning ‘to be unwilling / to refuse’; it governs the infinitival/finite action expressed by ببرد (‘to carry/take’). خویشتن functions as the reflexive ‘himself/with himself,’ while نام نیکی is a genitival phrase ‘a good name’ or ‘good reputation.’ The verb ببرد (take/carry) comes at the end, typical of Persian clause order.
segments.s758-g45: چنین است is a fixed verbless-like formula meaning ‘thus is’ or ‘such is.’ آیین is ‘custom/way/rule,’ and چرخ روان is a compound where چرخ (the Wheel/Heaven/Fate) is modified by روان (‘moving/flowing’). The noun + modifier order (چرخ روان) is common in poetry and yields ‘the moving wheel’ or ‘the wheel that moves’ (i.e., Fate/Heaven).
segments.s758-g46: توانا is a predicate adjective ‘powerful/capable’; به هر کار is a prepositional phrase ‘in/for every task’ (به + هر + کار). The contrastive و ما ناتوان links the preceding clause to ‘and we are powerless,’ where ناتوان is a predicate adjective with the copula implied (هستیم is omitted, as is common in poetry).
segments.s758-g9: مبادا is an optative/negative particle meaning 'may it not be' or 'lest' introducing a wished-for prevention. ز + گیتی (literally 'from the world') is a poetic way to say 'in the world' or 'anywhere in the world.' کسی مستمند is a noun phrase 'someone needy' (کسی = 'someone/anyone', مستمند = 'needy'). Word order is determiner/particle → place → indefinite person → predicate-adjective.
segments.s758-g10: که introduces a subordinate clause ('such that' / 'who/that'), linking the wished-for clause to the feared consequence. از درد او 'from his pain/suffering' uses از as 'from' and او as 'his' (3rd person). بر من آید گزند is a fixed phrasing meaning 'harm comes upon me' — آید is the present/subjunctive/past-present-form of آمد used narratively as 'befall/come' and گزند is the noun 'harm'. Word order is cause/source (از درد او) → dative/target (بر من) → verb (آید) → object/nominal result (گزند).
segments.s758-g11: ز کشور کنم دور بدخواه را is an accusative construction where کنم (1st-person verb 'I will do / I will make') governs the object بدخواه را. ز کشور کردن دور = 'to put (someone) far from the country' = 'banish/expel from the realm.' The object marker را follows بدخواه to mark it as definite. Verb comes before some objects here because of poetic inversion, but functionally 'I will make the ill-wisher distant from the country.'
segments.s758-g12: بر آیین شاهان کنم گاه را: بر آیین = 'according to / in the manner of' (بر used in the sense 'in accordance with'), آیین شاهان = 'the custom/practice of kings.' کنم (again 1st person) takes گاه را ('the time' with object marker را) as its object: 'I will set/establish the time.' The clause expresses a second purposeful action framed by royal custom. Word order here is prepositional phrase → genitive noun → verb → object with را (poetic inversion is common).
segments.s758-g13: نشانی ز پیروز خسرو بجست is a compact past narration. نشانی ('a sign/trace') is the object of بجست ('he sought' — past tense), and ز + پیروز خسرو means 'of/relating to Khosrow the Victorious' (ز as 'of/from'). Word order in classical Persian often places the object or its modifier before the verb (here the verb بجست ends the clause).
segments.s758-g14: بیاورد ناگاه مردی درست: بیاورد is a narrative third-person verb 'he brought' or 'he produced.' ناگاه ('suddenly') is an adverb modifying the verb. مردی درست is an indefinite noun phrase 'a proper/upright man' (مردی = 'a man', درست here = 'upright/proper'). The verb precedes the adverb and the noun phrase because of poetic word order, but the sense is 'suddenly he produced/brought a worthy man.'
segments.s758-g15: خبر چون به نزدیک پوران رسید uses چون in a temporal sense ('when'/'as'), so the clause reads 'when the news reached near Pouran.' به نزدیک پوران is a locative expression 'to/near Pouran' and رسید is the past tense 'reached/arrived.' Classical Persian commonly places the verb at the end of the clause.
segments.s758-g16: ز لشکر بسی نامور برگزید is a clause where برگزید ('he chose/selected') is the verb at the end. ز لشکر = 'from the army/host' marks the source, بسی here functions as an intensifier ('many/very' in classical usage) modifying نامور ('renowned/famed'), so بسی نامور = 'many famed ones' or 'very famed.' Together: 'he picked many renowned men from the host.'
segments.s758-g1: This is an introductory existence clause. یکی ('a certain') + noun (دختری, 'a maiden') sets up an indefinite subject; بود is the past copula 'there was'. پوران بنام is a postnominal name phrase: بنام ('called/named') follows the name, so the whole yields 'there was a maiden called Pūrān.'
segments.s758-g2: چو begins a temporal clause 'when'. زن شاه شد is 'she became the king's wife' (زن = wife, شاه = king, شد = became). The next clause, کارها گشت خام, uses two verbs in past (گشت 'became/turned') with کارها 'affairs' as subject and خام 'raw/unripe/ill‑handled' as predicate adjective; together: 'affairs became raw/immature.'
segments.s758-g3: بران here is a locative/temporal particle 'thereupon' or 'on that occasion'; تخت شاهیش = 'the royal throne' (شاهی + -ش/-یش marking the royal/possessive sense). بنشاندند is past plural 'they seated/installed (her)'; the verb is transitive and places the previously mentioned maiden on the throne.
segments.s758-g4: بزرگان is the subject 'the great ones/nobles'. گوهر افشاندند is a simple verb phrase 'they scattered/jewels they cast'. The short form برو here is poetic/archaic (functioning like بر او 'upon her' / 'before her'); the verb افشاندند 'they scattered' ends the clause, as usual in Persian.
segments.s758-g5: چنین گفت is a formulaic 'thus said' (چنین = 'thus/so'). پس 'then' places it in sequence; دخت پوران is the speaker. The conjunction/quotative که introduces reported speech/content ('that'), followed by the first word of her clause (من 'I'). Note: here گفت is the past verb 'said' (the provided lemma senses did not list the verbal past directly).
segments.s758-g6: نخواهم is the negative present/subjunctive form of خواستن and here functions as 'I will not / I do not wish to'. It is followed by the infinitive پراگندن ('to scatter/disperse') and then the object انجمن ('assembly, council'). Classical/poetic Persian often places the infinitive immediately after the auxiliary/finite verb, then the object.
segments.s758-g7: کسی را که is a relative/indefinite construction 'whoever / anyone who'. درویش باشد uses the subjunctive/present form باشد 'be' to state the condition 'is a dervish/poor person'. ز گنج ('from the treasure') gives the source—she promises to use the treasury to help them. Note the poetic particle ز = 'from'.
segments.s758-g8: توانگر کنم is a causative phrase 'I will make (someone) wealthy' (کنم = 'I do/make' used to form 'make X Y'). تانماند is the purpose/result clause 'ta nâmānad' = 'so that he will not remain' (negative of نماند). به رنج ('in suffering/hardship') completes the clause: 'so that he may not remain in hardship.'
segments.s758-g17: ببردند is a past-tense, 3rd-person plural verb ('they brought/took'); the phrase that follows names the person brought. پیروز here is a substantive/name meaning 'the victor' and functions as the direct object. راپیش اوی is a compressed sequence meaning 'before him' (را marking the definite object and پیش اوی = 'in front of him'); word order (verb — object — prepositional phrase) is typical of epic/poetic Persian.
segments.s758-g18: بدو گفت is literally 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him', گفت = 'said' here — an instance of reported speech). کای is the vocative 'O' addressing the addressee. بد تن is a compound phrase: بد qualifies تن ('person/self') and together means 'ill‑disposed/wicked person'. کینه‌جوی is an adjective formed by کینه + جوی ('one who seeks/looks for grudges'), i.e. 'vengeful' or 'grudge‑seeking'; جوی is the agentive/active sense 'seeking'.
segments.s758-g19: ز introduces the causal/instrumental sense 'for/on account of'. کاری که کردی is a noun plus relative clause 'the deed that you did' (کاری = 'deed/act', که کردی = 'that you did'). بیابی is a 2nd‑person form meaning 'you will find/receive' (subjunctive/future sense in poetic register), and جزا is the object 'retribution/punishment' placed after the verb in this poetic word order.
segments.s758-g20: چنانچون بود is a comparative/illustrative clause 'just as it was' (چنان‌چون = 'just as/like so', بود = past 'was'). در خور is an idiom meaning 'appropriate/deserving of' (در + خور), and ناسزا is the noun 'insult/abuse' that specifies what it is deserving of; the whole clause qualifies the punishment just stated.
segments.s758-g21: مکافات یابی 'you shall receive retribution' — مکافات = 'retribution/punishment' as the noun, یابی again the 2nd‑person verbal form (subjunctive/future force) 'you will receive/find', ز کرده = 'for/from the deed (that) you have done' (ز marks the cause), and کنون = 'now' sets the temporal frame.
segments.s758-g22: برانم is a 1st‑person verbal form (here 'I will cause/drive/bring about'); followed by ز گردن تو را—ز گردن 'from the neck', تو = 'you', and را is the object marker highlighting 'جوی خون' (a stream of blood) which follows. The structure is causative/resultative: 'I will (cause) a stream of blood to issue from your neck.'
segments.s758-g23: ز آخر هم آنگه is a sequence meaning 'at last; moreover then' (ز آخر = 'finally/at the end', هم آنگه = 'also/then'). یکی کره خواست = 'someone asked/wanted a foal' (یکی = 'one/a certain person', کره = 'calf/foal', خواست = past 'wanted/asked for'). The clause uses simple past narrative order.
segments.s758-g24: به زین اندرون = 'in/into the saddle' (به = 'to/at/on', زین = 'saddle', اندرون = 'inside'), followed by نوز نابوده راست describing the foal: نوز = 'newborn', نابوده here intends 'not destroyed/unhurt' (archaic/poetic use) and راست functions as a final predicate 'stood/was' in terse epic style. The adjective sequence follows the noun in poetic order.
segments.s758-g33: زدی is the past (narrative) form of زدن ‘to strike’ used here of a repeated action. هر زمان = “each time” (هر + زمان) places the temporal phrase before the verb, and خویشتن is a reflexive/emphatic object ‘himself.’ بر زمین = ‘onto the ground’ (preposition + noun). The clause literally reads ‘he struck himself onto the ground every time.’
segments.s758-g34: بران here functions as ‘on that / thereon’ (pointing back to the previous action). کره is the young animal ‘calf.’ بربود is a past passive / intransitive sense ‘was carried off / was taken away.’ چند آفرین is an exclamatory phrase: چند ‘several/many’ + آفرین ‘acclamation/“bravo”’ — ‘many cries of “afarin!”’
segments.s758-g35: چنین تا sets up a result or continuation ‘thus until…’. برو is a narrative/iterative present/past ‘go on / went on’ (classical Persian often uses such forms without auxiliaries). بر بدرید چرم = ‘they tore at / tore open the hide’ (بر + verb + object), with بدرید the perfective sense ‘to tear.’ The clause means ‘they continued until they tore the leather/hide.’
segments.s758-g36: همی‌رفت is the habitual/progressive past ‘was going / used to go’ (here iterative: ‘kept flowing’). خون ‘blood’ is the subject; از برش ‘from its cut’ (از = from; برش = the cut/wound). نرم نرم is a reduplicated adverb meaning ‘softly / gently’ (reduplication emphasises gradualness). The clause: ‘blood kept flowing gently from the wound.’
segments.s758-g37: سرانجام = ‘finally / in the end.’ جانش is the possessive/enclitic form meaning ‘his life.’ به خواری = ‘in humiliation’ (به + abstract noun خواری). به داد here reads as ‘to/into judgment or justice’ (به + داد), so the segment says ‘in the end his life, in humiliation, was handed over to judgment/justice.’
segments.s758-g38: چرا جویی is a nominal phrase ‘seeking (of) why / seeking justice’ (چرا = why; جویی = search/pursuit), here functioning interrogatively/retorically. از کار بیداد = ‘from the deeds/works of oppression’ (از = from; کار = deed; بیداد = oppression/injustice). داد at the end is used as the abstract noun ‘justice’ — the whole segment asks rhetorically who would expect justice from acts of oppression: ‘who would seek justice from the deeds of tyranny?’
segments.s758-g39: همی‌داشت is the habitual/past progressive ‘kept / retained’ (she continually had). این زن = ‘this woman’ (subject). جهان را (را marks the definite object) + به مهر = ‘with affection’ / ‘by love’ (به + مهر ‘love’). Literally: ‘this woman continually held the world by love.’
segments.s758-g40: نجست is the simple past ‘she leaped / sprang.’ از بر خاک = ‘from off the earth / from the dust’ (از + بر + خاک). باد here is used in the optative sense ‘may (it) …’ (a conventional wish/curse particle in classical Persian), and سپهر = ‘the sky / the heavens.’ The line reads as a bold poetic image: ‘she sprang up from the dust — (may) the heavens be stirred/blow.’

sentences.s758-s13: He bound it, placed upon it a weight like a stone, and threw it around the leopard's neck.
sentences.s758-s14: That lord of vengeance drew from the saddle that swift, unseen calf into the field.
sentences.s758-s15: He sent several horsemen into the field, with lassos looped over their saddle-cloths.
sentences.s758-s16: so that whenever you charged at his calf, from time to time you would throw him down.
sentences.s758-s21: After six months had passed in his affairs, suddenly his compass turned askew.
sentences.s758-s22: Within a week he fell ill and died; he was unwilling to carry his good name with himself.
sentences.s758-s23: Such is the custom of the moving Wheel (of Fate): it is powerful in every deed, and we are powerless.
sentences.s758-s5: May there not be any needy person in the world, lest harm come to me from his suffering.
sentences.s758-s6: I will drive the ill-wisher far from the realm, and according to the custom of kings I will set the time.
sentences.s758-s7: He sought a trace of Khosrow the Victorious, and suddenly brought forth a worthy man.
sentences.s758-s8: When the news reached Pouran's proximity, he selected many renowned men from the army.
sentences.s758-s1: There was a maiden called Pūrān; when she became the king's wife, affairs turned raw/ill‑managed.
sentences.s758-s2: They seated her on the royal throne; the nobles scattered jewels before her.
sentences.s758-s3: Then the maiden Pūrān said thus: 'I will not scatter the assembly.'
sentences.s758-s4: I will make anyone who is a dervish rich from the treasure, so that he will not remain in hardship.
sentences.s758-s9: They brought Pirooz before him, and he said to him, 'O wicked man, seeker of grudges.'
sentences.s758-s10: For the deed that you have done you shall find punishment, just as is fitting for an insult.
sentences.s758-s11: You shall receive retribution for what you have done now; I will make a stream of blood flow from your neck.
sentences.s758-s12: At last, then, someone asked for a foal; inside the saddle a newborn foal stood unharmed.
sentences.s758-s17: He struck himself down to the ground each time; on that the calf was carried off, and many cries of “afarin!” were raised.
sentences.s758-s18: Thus they went on until they tore the hide, and blood kept flowing gently from the wound.
sentences.s758-s19: At last his life was, in humiliation, handed over to judgment; who would seek justice from the deeds of oppression?
sentences.s758-s20: This woman kept the world by love; she sprang up from the dust — may the heavens blow (be stirred)!
